mcq.q: An Embedded System (ES) is best defined as a combination of hardware, software, and...

opts: It can be altered by the user

opts: ES applications are alterable; GP are not
ES key factor is performance; GP is cost
ES applications are non-alterable; GP are alterable
ES power consumption is high; GP is low
mcq.answer: ES applications are non-alterable; GP are alterable

mcq.q: For which system is 'Response Time' often critical?
Power, cost, speed, and size are known as:

opts: System Board (SB) and System on Chip (SoC)
mcq.answer: System Board (SB) and System on Chip (SoC)

mcq.q: Which technology integrates all system components (Processor, Memory, etc.) onto a single IC?

What does 'VLSI' stand for?
MPUs, MCUs, SoCs, and RAM are all examples of:

What component replaced large 'Vacuum Tubes' to create smaller 'Microprocessors'?
What is a 'Microcontroller' (MCU)?
opts: Just another name for a CPU
A small computer on a single IC
mcq.answer: A small computer on a single IC

mcq.q: What is the correct relationship between MPU and MCU?
opts: MCU is a part of MPU
MPU is a part of MCU
mcq.answer: MPU is a part of MCU

mcq.q: What is the function of a GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)?
opts: To assist the MP with graphics operations
mcq.answer: To assist the MP with graphics operations

mcq.q: Which secondary processor is a microprocessor that deals with 'Complex Computations'?

tf.q: An Embedded System is designed to perform a specific function.
An embedded system is a combination of hardware and software only.
tf.correction: It is a combination of hardware, software, and a mechanical component.

tf.q: A general-purpose computer (like a laptop) is an example of a 'specific purpose' system.
tf.correction: It is a 'general purpose' system.

tf.q: In an embedded system, applications are 'non-alterable' by the user.
'Performance' is the main key factor for embedded systems.
tf.correction: Application specific requirements (cost, power, size) are the key factors.

tf.q: A breadboard is used to build a System on Chip (SoC).
tf.correction: It is used to build a System Board (SB).

tf.q: A System on Chip (SoC) is less expensive and smaller than a System Board (SB).
VLSI is an IC with millions of transistors.
Old processors used transistors, while new microprocessors use vacuum tubes.
tf.correction: Old processors used vacuum tubes; new ones use transistors.

tf.q: A system can have multiple CPUs.
tf.correction: A system can have one CPU (primary processor).

tf.q: A Microcontroller (MCU) is a complete computer on a chip, containing a processor (MPU), memory, and I/O.
A DSP (Digital Signal Processor) assists the main processor with graphics operations.
tf.correction: The GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) assists with graphics.

Convert physical events to electrical signals
mcq.answer: Convert physical events to electrical signals

mcq.q: What is the function of an actuator?
opts: Convert electrical signals to physical events
Convert physical events to electrical signals
mcq.answer: Convert electrical signals to physical events

tf.q: The Control Unit (CU) is a component found inside the MP.
The ALU is used for complex computations like Fourier transform.
An MCU is a high-performance version of an SoC.
In BareMetal SW, the Application layer deals directly with the Kernel.
BareMetal SW is typically used for microcontrollers that do not have high performance.
A car is an example of a large system that can consist of at least 200 ECUs.
A sensor converts electrical signals into physical events.
An actuator takes input from the system and gives output to the environment.
SCADA is an example of a first-generation embedded system.
ASICs are designed for general-purpose use.
Miniaturization is a characteristic of fourth-generation embedded systems.
Medium-scale embedded systems usually contain an operating system.
For large-scale embedded systems, response time is not critical.
In Soft Real-time systems, missing a deadline is tolerated.
Event-triggered systems are dynamic and depend on the occurrence of events.

Which components does an ABS use to function?
opts: Wheel speed sensors and an ECU
mcq.answer: Wheel speed sensors and an ECU

tf.q: Washing machines and refrigerators are examples of automotive embedded systems.
ABS works by using an ECU to rapidly apply and release the brakes.
EEG and ECG machines are examples of embedded systems in the 'Telecom' category.
Data collected by an embedded system must always be stored; it cannot be deleted instantly.
Embedded data communication can only happen through a wired interface like Ethernet.
A 'Monitoring' system uses sensors to determine the state of variables.
An embedded system for 'Control' contains sensors but does not require actuators.
The 'Bottom-up' design process starts with system integration and moves toward requirements.
Requirements for an embedded system can only be functional.
Performance, cost, and physical size are examples of nonfunctional requirements.
Manufacturing cost includes the cost of components, assembly, and the design process (NRE).
Power consumption is usually specified by the customer in terms of allowable wattage.
In a requirements form, 'Inputs and outputs' is a simple entry covering only device types.
The most important power decision is often whether the machine will be battery-powered or wall-plugged.
The GPS moving map example specified a power requirement of 100mW.

Architecture design must only account for functional specifications.
In the GPS hardware, the 'Frame Buffer' manages input from the user interface.
It is recommended to start coding or designing circuits *before* architecting the system.
A microprocessor (MPU) is an independent device that does not require other chips.
A microcontroller (MCU) is considered a specific-purpose device.
'RF' stands for Register Files, which are groups of registers inside the microprocessor.
The Program Counter (PC) is a register located inside the Register Files.
C Code is directly converted into Machine Language (0s and 1s).
The Program Counter (PC) contains the address of the *current* instruction being executed.
After fetching an instruction, the CPU increments the PC to point to the next instruction.

It is easier to fetch an instruction to a register inside the CPU than to deal directly with the Bus.

Decoding an instruction involves sending a signal to the ALU.

tf.q: The Program Counter (PC) is a register located inside the Register Files.
The PC knows its initial location through the startup code.
The PC holds the memory address of the instruction *currently* being executed.
tf.correction: It holds the address of the next instruction.

tf.q: The Instruction Register (IR) is located inside the microprocessor to make it easier to deal with the instruction.
Decoding an instruction means understanding it and sending a signal to the ALU.
In modern microprocessors, all ALU results are stored in the Accumulator.
tf.correction: They are stored in any empty general purpose register.

tf.q: Every microprocessor uses the same machine language for the same operation.
tf.correction: Each microprocessor can have a different machine language.

tf.q: Assembly language speed advantage is no longer critical when using high-performance processors and compilers.
Understanding an instruction requires both Hardware (ID) and Software (Compiler).
CISC architecture understands *many* instructions and requires a *strong* compiler.
tf.correction: It requires a simple compiler because it understands many instructions directly.

tf.q: A 'Hard-Wired' ID is made of logic gates and is considered fast.
Increasing the number of instructions in a 'Hard-Wired' ID makes it cheaper to manufacture.
tf.correction: It makes it more expensive because it is hardware.

tf.q: A 'Memory-Mapped' ID takes more time because it searches for instructions in memory.
CISC systems generally lose time in the searching process.
RISC systems have high power consumption in the ALU compared to the ID.
tf.correction: ALU consumption is less; ID consumption is high (logic gates).

tf.q: RISC is designed to operate at higher speeds by performing a smaller number of instructions.
CISC architecture can only use Von-Neumann Architecture.
tf.correction: It can be Harvard or Von-Neumann.

tf.q: In RISC, adding more instruction types makes the microprocessor more complicated and slower.

What is the primary function of General Purpose Registers (GPR)?

mcq.answer: To store in GPR for faster access by being near to CPU

where is the Program Counter (PC) placed?

Control the flow of JMP instructions
mcq.answer: Store memory address of the last data element added to the stack

mcq.answer: A set of flags (bits) where each has a specific function

It only changes the Program Counter (PC) address
mcq.answer: It only changes the Program Counter (PC) address

opts: Slicing (dividing into more than one operation)
mcq.answer: Slicing (dividing into more than one operation)

tf.q: GPRs store ALU results temporarily until the CU stores them finally in RAM.
Using the 'register' keyword in C always guarantees that data will be stored in GPR.
tf.correction: It depends on the compiler and if a register is available.

tf.q: The Program Counter (PC) is classified as a Special Purpose Register (SPR).
The Stack Pointer (SP) stores the address of the first item added to the stack.
tf.correction: It stores the address of the last item added to the stack.

tf.q: The stack is a specific region of memory used for temporary data in a LIFO manner.
The Accumulator (ACC) is a primary register used in modern processors.
tf.correction: It is unavailable in modern microprocessors.

tf.q: The Instruction Register (IR) stores the instruction inside it after it is fetched from memory.
The Process Status Word (PSW) is a set of flags where each bit has a specific function.
Every instruction, including Jump (JMP), must be executed in the ALU.
tf.correction: JMP instructions alter the flow of execution without using the ALU.

tf.q: The INC instruction may not involve the ALU if the processor has specialized hardware for it.
An 8-bit architecture means the width of the memory register is 8-bits.
Slicing is the solution for handling operands that are larger than the ALU bit-width.
Special Purpose Registers (SPR) are also known as Special Function Registers (SFR).
The Sign flag bit represents the sign of the last result from the ALU.
Memory locations in an 8-bit architecture can hold any value other than 0 or 1.
tf.correction: Each bit in a location can only hold a value of 0 or 1.

mcq.answer: Data is transferred from SRAM to E2PROM

mcq.q: Why does NVRAM have a high cost per bit?
Which memory is considered 'OTP' (One Time Program)?

tf.q: ROM is the program memory that holds the code.
Access time of ROM is faster than RAM.
tf.correction: Access time of ROM is slower than RAM.

tf.q: A MOSFET acts like a switch controlling electric current flow.
In a MOSFET, current flows from source to drain when voltage is applied to the gate.
The floating gate in an FGM is directly connected to external circuits.
tf.correction: It is electrically isolated and not directly connected to any external circuit.

tf.q: Charge in the floating gate is trapped even if power is disconnected.
In FGM, a programmed state represents logic 1.
tf.correction: Programmed state (with electrons) represents logic 0.

tf.q: High voltage on the drain is used to erase the FGM by attracting negative charges.
The microprocessor can write code directly to ROM during normal operation.
tf.correction: ROM is Read-Only; code is written by a burner or flasher.

tf.q: Mask Programmable ROM bugs can be fixed using a software update.
tf.correction: There is no way to fix bugs in Mask ROM once manufactured.

tf.q: In PROM, a burned fuse represents logic 0.
EPROM data can be corrupted by radiation and noise.
EEPROM is erased using ultraviolet light.
tf.correction: EPROM uses UV; EEPROM (E2PROM) uses electricity.

tf.q: EEPROM has a higher endurance (100,000 cycles) compared to Flash (10,000 cycles).
Flash memory is more expensive per bit than EEPROM.
tf.correction: Flash has a lower cost per bit than EEPROM.

tf.q: Flash memory erasing must be done sector by sector, not byte by byte.
NVRAM combines the advantages of RAM (read/write) and ROM (non-volatile).
External EEPROM is connected to the CPU via master on the bus.
tf.correction: Internal EEPROM uses the bus; External EEPROM uses communication protocols.

tf.q: The BIOS chip is an example of Mask Programmable ROM.
In EPROM, covering the small window can help protect it from radiation corruption.

tf.q: Cache memory is based on DRAM technology.
tf.correction: It is based on SRAM.

tf.q: SRAM has a high cost per bit.
CPU speed is slower than RAM speed.
tf.correction: CPU speed is faster than RAM speed.

tf.q: When a CPU requests an address, the Cache fetches only that specific address.
tf.correction: It fetches that address and the next few addresses.

tf.q: A 'Cache Miss' means the CPU found the requested address in the Cache.
tf.correction: That is a Cache Hit; a Miss means it didn't find it.

tf.q: L3 Cache is faster than L1 Cache.
tf.correction: L1 is the fastest.

tf.q: Cache Coherence is needed when multiple cores cache the same address.
The solution to Cache Coherence involves one controller notifying the other about edits.
Register Files require buses for the CPU to access them.
tf.correction: CPU doesn't want buses to access Register Files.

tf.q: Register Files are faster than Cache memory.

opts: MPU does translation, MMU does protection
MPU does protection, MMU does translation

tf.q: An FPU provides higher precision and much faster performance for fractional math.
If a CPU lacks an FPU, it cannot perform floating-point arithmetic at all.
tf.correction: It can perform them in software, but it will be much slower.

tf.q: The MPU is physically placed between the cache and the RAM.
tf.correction: It is integrated inside the CPU/MCU, though conceptually it sits between them.

tf.q: Configuration registers in the MPU define address ranges and access permissions.
The Hypervisor layer helps in running Linux and RTOS independently on the same device.
A Bus Fault triggered by the MPU will stop the system.
MPU hardware split RAM into address ranges to prevent OS interference.
The MMU allows a system with 1GB RAM to run an app that believes it has 8GB.
Paging keeps all portions of an application in RAM simultaneously.
tf.correction: Only active portions currently needed are kept in RAM.

tf.q: Inactive pages in an MMU system are moved in and out of the disk as required.
A microcontroller can run Linux even if it doesn't have an MMU.
tf.correction: An MMU is a crucial requirement for installing Linux normally.

tf.q: The MPU provides improved reliability and security through fault detection.
If a memory region is defined as Read-Only by the MPU, no task can write to it.
The MMU maps virtual addresses used by applications into physical addresses in RAM.
Scientific computing and 3D simulations are examples of programs that benefit from an FPU.

mcq.q: Which signal is used by the CPU to read data from a peripheral like a keyboard?

What is the typical direction of the Address Bus in a computing system?
Which control signal indicates that the CPU wants to fetch an instruction from ROM?
What characterizes an 'Active Low' signal on a bus diagram?
What is the primary characteristic of the Von-Neumann architecture?
opts: Single shared bus for code and data
mcq.answer: Single shared bus for code and data

mcq.q: In Von-Neumann architecture, what term describes RAM, ROM, and I/O sharing the same address space?
Why is Von-Neumann architecture considered less expensive than Harvard?
opts: It requires fewer cables and buses
mcq.answer: It requires fewer cables and buses

mcq.q: In a digital signal diagram, what does a signal with *no* circle (bubble) at the gate indicate?
Which architecture suffers from a performance bottleneck because it cannot access RAM and ROM simultaneously?
How does Harvard architecture avoid the shared bus bottleneck?
opts: By using separate buses for instructions and data
mcq.answer: By using separate buses for instructions and data

mcq.q: Which architecture is generally easier to pipeline for higher performance?
According to the comparison table, which architecture has 'no chances' for accidental corruption of program memory?
Where is Harvard architecture commonly applied?
Which signal is activated when the CPU wants to send data to a printer?
What happens if a Von-Neumann CPU needs to fetch data while executing an instruction?
opts: It must wait and complete the cycles sequentially
It uses the Address bus for data
mcq.answer: It must wait and complete the cycles sequentially

mcq.q: Which feature is unique to Von-Neumann architecture according to the comparison?
What are the three main components of a computing system mentioned in the lecture?
What is the direction of the Data Bus when the CPU reads from ROM?
How is a component like ROM or RAM selected in Von-Neumann architecture?
Which signal is specifically for writing data to RAM?
In Harvard architecture, how does the CPU distinguish between RAM and ROM addresses?
Which architecture wastes more time due to single bus access?
What is the primary reason for using Von-Neumann in Personal Computers?
opts: It avoids cable complexity in large systems
mcq.answer: It avoids cable complexity in large systems

mcq.q: How are the buses organized in a standard connection?
opts: Address, Data, and Control bus set
mcq.answer: Address, Data, and Control bus set

mcq.q: What part of the system 'points' to the location to be accessed?
What does MWTC stand for?
What is the input/output type for Harvard architecture according to the diagram?
Which architecture is described as having a parallel connection?
According to the connection diagrams, the CPU connects to memory using how many types of buses?

tf.q: In Von-Neumann architecture, the CPU can access RAM and ROM at the same time.
tf.correction: It uses a single shared bus and cannot access them simultaneously.

tf.q: The Data Bus is bi-directional when connecting to RAM.
The Address Bus is bi-directional between the CPU and peripherals.
tf.correction: It is uni-directional from the microprocessor to memory/peripherals.

tf.q: Active Low signals are active when their value is 0.
Harvard architecture requires more cables and buses than Von-Neumann.
MWTC stands for Memory Read Control.
tf.correction: It stands for Memory Write Control.

tf.q: Harvard architecture has higher performance than Von-Neumann due to parallel access.
In Von-Neumann architecture, I/O devices share the same address range logic as RAM and ROM.
Self-modifying code is possible in Harvard architecture.
tf.correction: It is possible only in Von-Neumann architecture.

tf.q: An 'Active High' signal is represented by a circle on the gate in a diagram.
tf.correction: The circle represents 'Active Low'; no circle represents 'Active High'.

tf.q: Pipelining is easier to implement in Harvard architecture.
Von-Neumann architecture is more expensive than Harvard architecture.
tf.correction: Harvard is more expensive due to more buses and parallel connections.

tf.q: Accidental corruption of program memory is a higher risk in Von-Neumann architecture.
IORC stands for I/O Read Control.
Harvard architecture is typically used in Personal Computers.
tf.correction: Von-Neumann is used in PCs; Harvard is used in Microcontrollers.

mcq.q: What are the two ways to connect Input/Output using Harvard Architecture?
Which I/O connection method is commonly used in Embedded Systems?
In Memory-Mapped I/O, which assembly instructions are used to deal with I/O?
In Memory-Mapped I/O, the code is typically written in which language before conversion?
In Memory-Mapped architecture, can the CPU deal with RAM and I/O at the same time?
opts: No, they are connected like Von Neumann
mcq.answer: No, they are connected like Von Neumann

mcq.q: Which instruction is used to deal with I/O in Port-Mapped architecture?
Which instruction is used to deal with ROM in Port-Mapped architecture?
How does the CPU distinguish addresses in Port-Mapped architecture?
opts: By the specific assembly instruction used
mcq.answer: By the specific assembly instruction used

mcq.q: In the '2nd way' (hybrid) architecture, using Bus no. 1 (Memory Mapped style) results in:
opts: Slower performance and no pipeline between RAM and I/O
Ability to use assembly In/Out instructions
Simultaneous access to RAM and I/O
mcq.answer: Slower performance and no pipeline between RAM and I/O

mcq.q: If dealing with I/O using Bus set no. 3 in the '2nd way' architecture, the program must be written in:
Which architecture is typically used in Personal Computers (PCs)?
Which architecture is typically used in Microcontrollers?
Why is Von Neumann suitable for PCs regarding signal length?
opts: It uses more wires for speed
It reduces the huge number of wires required for large scale systems
It allows wires to be wireless
mcq.answer: It reduces the huge number of wires required for large scale systems

mcq.q: In a PC, the microprocessor loads the OS from the Hard Disk into:
Why is Harvard Architecture suitable for Microcontrollers?
opts: Components are on one small chip allowing small wires
It is cheaper for large scale
mcq.answer: Components are on one small chip allowing small wires

mcq.q: What are the three stages of a standard instruction cycle mentioned?
What technique allows the CPU to fetch a 2nd instruction while decoding the 1st?
Which architecture supports Instruction Pipelining?
Why can Von Neumann NOT support pipelining?
opts: It cannot store and fetch instructions at the same time
mcq.answer: It cannot store and fetch instructions at the same time

mcq.q: How does Pipelining affect execution time?
What does MIPS stand for?
Which type of processor architecture supports pipelining?
Why does CISC generally NOT support pipelining?
opts: Instructions are executed in one cycle
Instructions can take more than one cycle to execute
mcq.answer: Instructions can take more than one cycle to execute

mcq.q: Most instructions in RISC are executed in how many clock cycles?

tf.q: In Memory-Mapped I/O, the CPU uses specific In/Out instructions.
tf.correction: It uses Load/Store instructions.

tf.q: In Port-Mapped architecture, the CPU can deal with RAM, ROM, and I/O simultaneously.
tf.correction: They are connected via different bus sets.

tf.q: Port-Mapped architecture relies on the assembly instruction type to identify the target (RAM, ROM, or I/O).
tf.correction: Load/Store for RAM, Read/Write for ROM, In/Out for I/O.

tf.q: Writing code in C-Language for I/O is associated with the 'In/Out' assembly instructions.
tf.correction: C-Language converts to Load/Store (Memory-Mapped style).

tf.q: Von Neumann architecture is used in PCs because it reduces the number of wires required.
tf.correction: A shared bus reduces wiring complexity for large scale systems.

tf.q: Microcontrollers typically use Von Neumann architecture.
tf.correction: Microcontrollers typically use Harvard architecture.

tf.q: Pipelining allows the Fetch, Decode, and Execute stages of different instructions to overlap.
tf.correction: This parallel processing increases performance.

tf.q: Von Neumann architecture supports pipelining because it can access RAM and ROM simultaneously.
tf.correction: Von Neumann cannot access both simultaneously, so it does not support pipelining.

tf.q: RISC processors execute most instructions in one clock cycle.
tf.correction: This feature supports pipelining.

tf.q: 8 MIPS means the processor can execute 8 thousand instructions per second.
tf.correction: It means 8 Million Instructions Per Second.

mcq.q: According to the lecture, if an arithmetic operation requires more than 8 bits in an 8-bit system, what happens?
opts: It is divided into multiple arithmetic operations
mcq.answer: It is divided into multiple arithmetic operations

mcq.q: What type of instructions does the 'I' in MIPS specifically refer to?

Which method can be used to generate a clock square wave using software?
What is the primary function of the clock in an embedded system?
opts: To provide power to the ALU
To control the system and keep speed consistent
To detect bit errors in the data bus
mcq.answer: To control the system and keep speed consistent

Which of the following is a type of mechanical clock system material?
Which clock system has the lowest manufacturing cost?
Which clock system provides the highest accuracy?
What does 'Settling Time' refer to in clock systems?
opts: The time to reach maximum voltage
The time for a signal to become stable with same frequency
The time it takes to power off the system
The duration of one square wave cycle
mcq.answer: The time for a signal to become stable with same frequency

mcq.q: Which system has the lowest settling time?
Which system has the lowest noise immunity against temperature?
Which system has high immunity against vibration noise?
Why do Crystal Oscillators have low immunity against vibration?
opts: Because they are made of metal

mcq.q: Which clock system has 'In between' cost and accuracy?
In the comparison table, which system has the highest settling time?
The speed of a RISC microprocessor depends on what?
opts: The size of the hard disk

tf.q: In an n-bit microcontroller, the bit size determines the location of the memory.
tf.correction: The n-bit data bus determines the location of the memory.

tf.q: MIPS stands for Millions Instructions Per Second.
A clock signal's frequency is inconsistent and constantly changing.
tf.correction: The clock frequency is constant.

tf.q: 8 MHz means the clock performs 8 thousand cycles per second.
tf.correction: It means 8 million cycles per second.

tf.q: In RISC architectures, one instruction typically takes one clock cycle.
An RC oscillator uses a 555 timer circuit with a resistor and capacitor.
Crystal oscillators are the lowest cost option for generating a clock.
tf.correction: RC oscillators are the lowest cost; Crystal oscillators are the highest cost.

tf.q: Ceramic resonators have high immunity against temperature effects.
Natural materials used for clocks take electrical power as input and output a square wave.

mcq.q: To reach each register in a peripheral, the formula used is:

opts: To make peripherals appear in a continuous address range

tf.q: In embedded systems, the connection for I/O is almost always memory-mapped.
Port-mapped I/O is the primary method used in Embedded Systems according to the lecture.
tf.correction: Memory-mapped I/O is used.

tf.q: A base address is used to reach each individual register in a peripheral.
tf.correction: Base address reaches the first address; Offset reaches each register.

tf.q: The System Bus must have a slave port to connect to the CPU's master port.
Registers in a peripheral are used to write 0 or 1 to perform actions like opening/closing a device.
A Technical Reference Manual (TRM) contains the bus addresses and register offsets for an ES engineer.
A Bus Matrix is used because Harvard architecture output is naturally port-mapped.
An Interrupt is a hardware or software signal that causes the processor to stop its current operation.
Polling is more efficient than interrupts because it gives attention to high-priority tasks immediately.
tf.correction: Interrupts handle high-priority tasks; Polling checks status continuously in a loop.

tf.q: Verilog code can define how many masters and slaves exist in a system bus.
In a Harvard CPU, the program memory and data memory share the same bus.
tf.correction: They have separate buses.

tf.q: The 'Base Address' is specifically used to reach the very first address of a peripheral.
The Output Data Register (ODR) for GPIO in the provided example has an offset of OX0.
tf.correction: The DR has offset OX0; the ODR has offset OX4.

tf.q: The CPU, GPU, and DSP can all act as masters on a system bus like AXI.
Interrupts are generally less efficient than polling because the CPU must wait for the device.
tf.correction: Interrupts are more efficient because they only give attention when needed.

mcq.q: What mechanism temporarily halts a program to attend to a high-priority task?
What is the special routine executed by the processor when an interrupt signal is received?
Where does the processor return after the ISR finishes execution?
opts: To the start of the program

mcq.q: Which method continuously checks the status of another device in a loop?
What is an example of an operation that uses polling?
opts: Checking a serial port for data in a loop
mcq.answer: Checking a serial port for data in a loop

mcq.q: Which benefit of interrupts prevents the need for constant status checking?
Interrupts facilitate the execution of multiple tasks without needing multiple processors, which is called:
Internal interrupts are typically generated by which component?
What table maps each interrupt source to its corresponding ISR?
What action involves saving the registers and program counter before jumping to an ISR?
In the interrupt processing flow, what happens immediately after the interrupt signal occurs?
A button press or sensor data availability is classified as what type of interrupt?
Which interrupts are initiated by program commands or predefined time intervals?
What type of interrupt can be disabled by the processor during critical sections?
Which of the following is an example of a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)?
What is a key characteristic required of an ISR to minimize latency?
Why are interrupts generally disabled during the execution of an ISR?
What happens when a higher-priority interrupt occurs while a lower-priority ISR is running?
opts: The higher-priority interrupt preempts the lower-priority ISR
mcq.answer: The higher-priority interrupt preempts the lower-priority ISR

mcq.q: What prevents all interrupts from being processed for a specific period?
Which specific timer resets the system in case of a software malfunction?
In the timer interrupt example, what must be enabled globally before execution?
What is defined as the time between the arrival of an interrupt and the execution of the ISR?
Which factor increases interrupt latency?
In the simple embedded system example, which event updates the button state?
What does the processor save during Context Saving?

tf.q: Polling is a mechanism that temporarily halts the normal execution of a program.
tf.correction: Interrupts halt the program; Polling is continuous checking in a loop.

tf.q: Real-time response allows a system to respond to external events like sensor data immediately.
The Interrupt Vector Table maps interrupt sources to their corresponding ISR.
Context switching occurs after the ISR has completed execution.
tf.correction: Context is saved before jumping to ISR and restored after completion.

tf.q: Software interrupts are initiated by hardware devices like sensors.
tf.correction: Hardware interrupts are initiated by devices; Software interrupts are initiated by commands.

tf.q: Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI) can be disabled by the processor during critical code sections.
tf.correction: NMIs cannot be disabled and are used for critical events like power failure.

tf.q: ISRs should avoid long delays and blocking I/O operations.
Higher-priority interrupts can preempt lower-priority ISRs.
Interrupt latency is affected by context switching and ISR length.
Disabling interrupts is often used when updating shared resources.

In UART, what is used to check the integrity of incoming bytes?
Communication where data is sent bit by bit, one after another, is called:
Why is parallel communication less commonly used in embedded systems?
opts: It is more complex and costly
mcq.answer: It is more complex and costly

mcq.q: Which protocol is described as 'fault-tolerant' and used in safety-critical systems?

Which protocol provides both high-speed data transfer and power to connected devices?
What is the typical range for a standard Bluetooth protocol?

If a device runs on battery, which protocol version is recommended?
Which protocol is best if you need to send a lot of data quickly?
Standard I2C speed is defined as:
How many masters does the SPI bus support?

tf.q: Communication interfaces are necessary for sub-systems to talk to the external world.
RS232 C is an example of an on-board communication interface.
tf.correction: RS232 C is an external communication interface.

tf.q: The I2C bus is a uni-directional serial link.
tf.correction: I2C is a bi-directional 2-wire serial bus.

tf.q: I2C includes a provision for collision detection.
SPI requires 4 wires: MOSI, MISO, SCK, and SS.
Full duplex mode means a device can send and receive data at the same time.
SPI provides automatic error checking during data transfer.
tf.correction: There is no provision for error checking in SPI.

tf.q: UART converts parallel data from a computer into a serial bit stream.
UART uses a clock signal to synchronize data between devices.
tf.correction: UART is asynchronous and does not need a clock signal.

tf.q: Serial communication uses more wires than parallel communication.
tf.correction: Serial uses fewer wires; parallel uses more.

tf.q: The CAN protocol is fault-tolerant and continues working even if there is an issue.
Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) features high power consumption.
tf.correction: BLE features low power consumption.

tf.q: UART and SPI are good choices for communicating over long distances.
tf.correction: They are good choices for short distances.

tf.q: I2C is often used to connect temperature sensors or LCD screens.
The I2C SDA line stands for Serial Data Line.
